query: common radiographic techniques for working length determination

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Length -- Other Issues -- Radiology
retrieved_docs.content: The distance between an x-ray source and an image receptor affects the focal length and determines the magnification and resolution of the resulting image. When the x-ray source is closer to the image receptor, the focal length is shorter, resulting in increased image magnification. Conversely, a longer focal length leads to reduced magnification. The size of the focal spot, the area on the x-ray tube from where x-rays are emitted, is another important consideration related to focal length in radiography. A smaller focal spot size allows for improved spatial resolution and finer detail in the radiographic image. However, a smaller focal spot size typically corresponds to a longer focal length, which can result in decreased magnification. Choosing the appropriate focal length and focal spot size allows radiologists to achieve the desired level of image magnification and resolution for accurate diagnosis. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Technical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on how a radiograph is taken, different tissues and anatomical structures may appear to have different attenuation and may even alter the size and location of organs. Because of this, it is important to thoroughly evaluate whether or not a radiograph taken is technically adequate so that accurate clinical decisions can be made.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Clinical Significance -- Magnification
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of heart magnification is important for evaluating cardiac-related pathology (eg, dilated cardiomyopathy, pericardial effusion, etc) To properly assess whether or not a heart is truly enlarged, one must consider what type of chest radiograph is (eg, PA or AP), as on AP views, the heart may appear larger than it is. One may also look at past chest radiographs to determine if there has been a significant change in the heart's transverse diameter over time (1 to 2 cm increase in diameter over a year). Another way to help determine if cardiomegaly is present is to look at a lateral view chest radiograph to see if one is available to reduce the size of the retrocardiac space.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Magnification
retrieved_docs.content: Magnification refers to how large the heart appears on a chest radiograph. One can quickly and conveniently determine whether the heart is the proper size by assessing the cardiothoracic ratio (CTR). The cardiothoracic ratio is determined by measuring the heart's widest diameter on a chest radiograph and then measuring the thoracic cage's widest internal diameter, usually directly above the diaphragm. The heart's measured diameter is then divided by the thoracic cage's diameter to yield the CTR. [19] Generally speaking, a CTR of less than 0.5 is considered normal, although some research has questioned this convention, suggesting that normal CTR should be lower. [20] Other research has suggested using measurements in centimeters to assess normal heart size, stating that a heart with a transverse diameter of 15cm or an increase in the transverse diameter of 1 to 2 cm over a year can be called cardiomegaly. It is also important to point out that just because the heart does not appear enlarged on a chest radiograph does not mean there may not be an underlying cardiac pathology. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Orthopedic Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Magnification increases the radiation dose as well as the scatter. The need for magnification is limited by digital zooming and the use of large displays. An increase of scattered radiation is seen when the gantry position is more than 30 degrees in left or right anterior oblique angulations or 15 degrees in cranial angulation. Additional exposure can be due to incorrect coordination between radiographers and surgeons due to non-standard means of communication. Effective methods of communication with accurate terminology must be developed between the surgical team and C-arm technicians. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology, Image Production and Evaluation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Image noise, another component that affects image quality, gives images a grainy appearance and causes random variations of image brightness. Factors that can contribute to image noise include a variation of photon concentration, thermal activity within an electronic device, fluorescent lights, or sensitivity of the image receptor. Noise can be controlled but not completely prevented. Objects with low contrast are typically most affected. [6] Artifacts are image features that are not truly present but appear due to the abnormality of the imaging modality. While artifacts do not affect the visibility of an object, they can be misinterpreted as anatomical structures or block important structures within the body. Artifacts can result from foreign materials, lead markers, loose filters in the X-ray tube, patient movements, or improper handling and film processing. [7] Distortion of an image is any misrepresentation or inaccurate impression of the target structure. Size distortion is magnifying the anatomical structure and is usually caused by an increased object-to-image receptor distance or decreased distance between the source of radiation and the image receptor. This phenomenon causes the object to look larger than it is in reality. Shape distortion refers to the elongation or shortening of the target object. This appearance results from improper angulation of the image receptor or axis or by technical and/or structural errors of the X-ray tube. [8] These factors should be avoided, if possible, to minimize mistakes in diagnosing a patient.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Five Anatomic Technical Assessments of Chest Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: Some of the more common criteria to assess radiographs, specifically chest radiographs, are penetration, inspiration, rotation, magnification, and angulation. "PIRMA" (penetration, inspiration, rotation, magnification, angulation) is a helpful mnemonic that can help one remember these 5 criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Clinical Significance -- Labeling
retrieved_docs.content: Suppose the incorrect name is placed on a radiograph. In that case, one might diagnose disease in a nondiseased patient or be unable to tell whether the pathology seen on a radiograph is acute or chronic. An example of this is observing Kerley B lines on a chest radiograph. Kerley B lines are 1- to 2-cm lines perpendicular to the pleural surface and extending out towards it, caused by the thickening of the subpleural interlobular septa. These lines are not specific to any disease (eg, they may be seen in a patient with acute heart failure exacerbation or chronic interstitial lung disease.) Correct exam dates can help ascertain the chronicity of this finding and help determine how to manage the patient clinically. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction -- Spatial Resolution
retrieved_docs.content: Spatial resolution is the imaging system's ability to distinguish the adjacent structures separate from each other. A bar pattern containing alternate radio-dense bars and radiolucent spaces of equal width can be imaged to get the subjective measurement of spatial resolution in units of line pairs per millimeter. The modulation transfer function (MTF) is an objective measurement of the spatial resolution obtained by measuring the transfer of signal amplitude of various spatial frequencies from object to image. [3] MTF is the best way to measure spatial resolution. The factors affecting spatial resolution include magnification, X-ray focal spot size, detector resolution, patient motion, and image processing. A limiting system spatial resolution of 2.5 mm or higher is essential for digital radiographs. [3] In the CR system, scattering of the laser beam during image readout is the primary factor limiting the spatial resolution. In DR systems, the spread of light photons when converting X-ray photons to light and detector element (del) size are the most important determinants of spatial resolution. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- Magnification
retrieved_docs.content: Magnification comes into play with this concept because depending on how the patient is positioned during the radiograph, the heart can appear slightly different. To grasp this concept, one must remember that objects closer to the X-ray beam in a radiograph appears larger when projected onto the digital detector. Chest X-rays can be taken in multiple ways, including the posterior-anterior (PA) and anterior-posterior (AP). PA radiographs are taken with the patient's posterior elements closest to the X-ray beam and their anterior elements closest to the digital detector. PA chest radiographs are generally considered the best reflection of a patient's true heart size, as the heart is close to the digital detector and does not get magnified as much. However, sick patients in the hospital, especially in the ICU, may not have PA radiographs performed and must have an AP radiograph done at the bedside. In AP radiographs, the heart is closer to the X-ray beam, and there is more magnification of the heart, making it appear larger than it is. [21] An easy way to remember the above concept relating PA/AP films to the distance from the X-ray beam is to remember that the naming of the radiograph describes where the X-ray beam enters then exits the patient (eg, for PA films, the X-ray beam enters posteriorly and exits anteriorly), the heart is an anterior structure. The closer to the X-ray beam the heart is, the more magnified it appears on a radiograph.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Discography Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: At this point, the C-arm is usually rotated to a lateral view, magnification visualizing the anterior and posterior aspect of the disk and spinal canal is obtained. The stylet is removed from the needle while not advancing or retracting the needle.  The cap on the end of the short IV extension of the contrast syringe is removed and attached to the needle that is in the disc.  The physician is now ready for the provocative portion of the discogram.  Multiple "sham" injections are usually performed.  The syringe is held and sometimes even tapped while asking the patient if they feel anything. If a pain response is elicited at this stage, it should be noted as a sham response. Otherwise, pain provocation at this stage should not occur. If using manometry, the manometer should read zero pressure and zero volume at this point.

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Length -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Focal length calculations are important when managing common eye conditions such as cataracts and evaluating patients for refractive surgical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluoroscopy Discography Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Under real-time fluoroscopy, the contrast is administered, and initial flow into the disc is noted.  If using a manometer, the opening pressure and volume are noted. Pressure and gradually increased, and pain location and intensity are noted.  The terms concordant and discordant are often used.  The volume at which pain may change from concordant to discordant is noted if such a change occurs.  While a manual discogram has no pressure transducer, volumes can still be reasonably noted at different times of the procedure.  Likewise, manometer volumes and pressures are noted as well.  Basic Dallas classification I-IV should be possible to discern on the lateral view, especially later on diagnostic quality lateral plain films.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Clinical Significance -- Angulation
retrieved_docs.content: The main artifacts seen in apical lordotic views and lordotic projections are elevation of the diaphragm, poor visualization of the lung bases, loss of definition of the aortic knob, and widening of mediastinal structures. [17] These observations in the apical lordotic view are from the X-ray beam's angle, which, instead of entering the patient parallel with the floor, enters the patient at an inclination above parallel. Taking into consideration the effects of magnification on anterior and posterior structures of the chest, it makes sense that if the angle at which the X-ray beam enters the patient is altered, altering the relative distance between different structures within the chest to the X-ray beam and digital detector, that structures  appears differently. The lungs and diaphragm bases get closer to the X-ray beam, and the apices of the lungs get further away from the X-ray beam in this type of view. In addition to the above-mentioned clues as to whether or not a radiograph is apical lordotic, one can also assess the clavicles. In a normal chest radiograph, the clavicles are a relative "S-shape," the medial ends should be approximately at the level of the anterior second and third ribs. The clavicles appear more straight in an apical lordotic view, and the medial ends may appear above the first rib.

retrieved_docs.title: CT Instrumentation and Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Summary and additional points to consider
retrieved_docs.content: Pitch is the ratio of the distance the table moved in a single x-ray tube rotation divided by the beam width. A beam width of 1 will have no slice overlap. A pitch greater than 1 will have slice gaps, while a less than 1 will overlap. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Production Technical Evaluation -- Issues of Concern -- X-Ray Production
retrieved_docs.content: The accumulation of electrons at the cathode results in a large negative charge that accelerates electrons toward the anode target. The cathode of an X-ray tube is made of a tungsten filament. X-ray tubes tend to have 2 pieces of tungsten per anode (dual-focus tubes), one measuring 1 cm in length and the second measuring 2 cm in length. The smaller the filament, the smaller the current the filament can handle and the fewer electrons the filament can produce. The smaller filament is used to take radiographs of smaller body parts. The larger filament is used for imaging of larger body parts. Because of the increased amount of electrons in the beam, there is increased repulsion amongst the beam's electrons, creating scattering of the beam and decreased spatial resolution of radiographs. This is corrected by having a negatively charged focusing cap at the end of the cathode, which repels the electrons away from the peripheral areas and creates a very narrow, focused electron beam, increasing spatial resolution. Now that the electrons have been excited and emitted from the tungsten filaments, they are accelerated toward the positively charged anode.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a reference point for several more advanced computed tomography examinations. Both Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative mappings derived from a routine chest x-ray, indicating that these comprehensive CT studies may be ordered when a standard chest x-ray is insufficient. Additionally, Computerized tomography of chest is identified as the primary mapping from a routine chest x-ray, reflecting its role as the most direct CT extension of the basic chest radiograph. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate imaging modalities, progressing from a routine chest x-ray to targeted CT imaging of the chest alone or combined brain‑chest assessment, with or without contrast, based on diagnostic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2416
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference for several computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a dedicated Computerized tomography of chest is mapped, indicating a direct relationship between the standard chest radiograph and the more detailed CT chest study. In addition, the routine chest x-ray is also linked, through other mappings, to more extensive CT examinations that combine multiple anatomical regions: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium. These connections illustrate that the routine chest x-ray not only underpins isolated chest CT imaging but also informs broader, multi‑region CT protocols, both with and without the use of intravenous contrast, highlighting its central role in guiding advanced diagnostic imaging strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is identified as a method_of, meaning it serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action, providing a structured approach for using CT scans in follow‑up assessments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for several advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. The CT scan of the chest is primarily mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating it is the main derivative imaging study. In addition, CT examinations that include the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, as well as CT scans encompassing the brain, chest, and abdomen, are also linked to the routine chest x‑ray through other mappings, reflecting supplementary or alternative relationships. These connections illustrate how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a foundational study for more comprehensive, multi‑region CT imaging, sometimes enhanced with contrast agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: The routine chest x-ray is used as a reference point for more extensive imaging examinations, with both computerized tomography of the brain and chest and computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen being considered alternative or mapped extensions of the routine chest x-ray. This relationship highlights how a basic chest radiograph can serve as a basis for selecting broader CT studies that include additional anatomical regions such as the brain, chest, and abdomen, informing clinical decisions about when more detailed cross‑sectional imaging may be warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2608

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2375
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is performed using plain X‑ray techniques. Computerized tomography of chest is considered the primary imaging modality that is mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, indicating that chest CT often follows or expands upon findings from a standard chest radiograph. In addition, computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen is another imaging study that is also mapped from the routine chest x‑ray, representing an alternative or supplementary advanced imaging approach that can be pursued when a broader anatomic assessment is required. These relationships illustrate how plain X‑ray techniques underlie routine chest x‑ray, which in turn serves as a reference point for both focused chest CT and more extensive CT examinations of the brain, chest, and abdomen.
